narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, a key factor in admission decisions. The model estimated your probability of failing the bar exam at 55.9%, which tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors in your profile, ranked by their influence on the outcome. The strongest factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your choice to attend part-time, which is uncommon—only about 7% of students do so—while nearly 93% go full-time, and the model sees this as a significant hurdle linked to lower bar passage rates. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 33.50, though below the average of 36.70, worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other academic strength. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA provided a meaningful boost toward passing, countering some risks but not enough to overcome the top concern. Compared to typical students, your middle family income bracket aligns with the norm, and your school deciles of 5th in Year 1 and 4th in Year 3 are a bit below average, but these played lesser roles here. While your background as a white male doesn't directly sway the model, it fits within common profiles where academic and enrollment choices dominate. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time commitment, as that could dramatically shift the prediction—perhaps retake the LSAT to push closer to or above average, or bolster your profile with additional experiences demonstrating bar readiness. Many students in similar spots succeed by addressing these exact levers, so reviewing your options with an advisor could open new paths.
